inputs.story: Julia has hired several new people to work as ride-managers for Walton World Amusement Park. She needs to check on their results after their first full day at work. Help her match each new employee to their ride and section of the park (broken down by color), as well as the number of people each served during that day. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
inputs.constraints:
1. The worker who works in the green section served more riders than Brett.
2. Peter served 25 fewer riders than Willis.
3. Brett served more riders than Peter.
4. The person who works in the yellow section served 25 more riders than the worker who works in the orange section.
5. Peter is either the person who served 125 riders or the person who works in the blue section.
inputs.constants:
employees: Brett; Peter; Victor; Willis.
riders: 50 riders; 75 riders; 100 riders; 125 riders.
section: blue; green; orange; yellow.
inputs.solution:
Riders	Employees	Section
50 riders	Peter	blue
75 riders	Willis	orange
100 riders	Brett	yellow
125 riders	Victor	green

asp_generation.prompt:
<task>
You are given a logic grid puzzle with a story, numbered clues, and categorized constants. Your task is to generate a complete ASP (Answer Set Programming) program that, when solved with Clingo, yields a unique answer set representing the puzzle's solution.

Provided below are:
- two examples: `example_1` and `example_2`,
- the problem you must solve: `problem_to_solve`,
- important rules: `rules`.
</task>

<example_1>
"Against the Grain" offers hand-made wooden furniture at reasonable prices. Each item is made by an in-house employee. Using only the clues that follow, match each item to the employee who crafted it, and determine its price and the type of wood used to make it. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. Bonita's piece costs $325.
2. The item made of poplar costs more than Yvette's piece.
3. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
4. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.

Constants:
Employees: Bonita; Yvette; Tabitha.
Prices: $225; $275; $325.
Wood types: ash; poplar; sandalwood.

ASP Program:
```asp
% Constants
employee("Bonita"; "Yvette"; "Tabitha").
price(225; 275; 325).
wood_type("ash"; "poplar"; "sandalwood").

% Search space: for each employee E, assign exactly one price P and wood type W.
{match(E, P, W): price(P), wood_type(W)}=1 :- employee(E).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{E1=E2; P1=P2; W1=W2}=0 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), (E1,P1,W1)!=(E2,P2,W2).

% 1. Bonita's piece costs $325.
P=325 :- match(E,P,W), E="Bonita".

% 2. The item made of poplar costs more than Yvette's piece.
P1>P2 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), W1="poplar", E2="Yvette".

% 3. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
P1=P2-50 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), E1="Tabitha", W2="sandalwood".

% 4. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.
{W="ash"; E="Yvette"}=1 :- match(E,P,W), P=275.

#show match/3.
```
</example_1>

<example_2>
The Winter Olympics have just wrapped up in Norway. Using only the clues that follow, determine the number of gold and silver medals won by each country. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. The four teams are the team from Bolivia, the team that won 3 gold medals, the team that won 6 silver medals, and the team from Argentina.
2. The team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals are different.
3. The team from Oman finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.
4. The squad that won 3 gold medals, the squad that won 6 silver medals and the squad from Bolivia were all different teams.
5. Neither the team from Argentina nor the squad that won 2 silver medals is the squad that won 4 gold medals.
6. The squad that won 2 gold medals is either the squad that won 6 silver medals or the team from Grenada.

Constants:
Countries: Argentina; Bolivia; Grenada; Oman.
Silver medals: 2; 6; 10; 11.
Gold medals: 1; 2; 3; 4.

ASP Program:
```asp
% Constants
country("Argentina"; "Bolivia"; "Grenada"; "Oman").
silver_medals(2; 6; 10; 11).
gold_medals(1; 2; 3; 4).

% Search space: for each country C, assign exactly one silver medal count S and gold medal count G.
{assign(C, S, G): silver_medals(S), gold_medals(G)}=1 :- country(C).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{C1=C2; S1=S2; G1=G2}=0 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), (C1,S1,G1)!=(C2,S2,G2).

% 1. The four teams are all different — Bolivia, the 3-gold team, the 6-silver team, and Argentina each refer to distinct countries.
{C="Bolivia"; G=3; S=6; C="Argentina"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G).

% 2. The team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals are different.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), C1="Oman", S2=10.

% 3. The team from Oman finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.
{G=2; G=1}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), C="Oman".

% 4. The squad that won 3 gold medals, the squad that won 6 silver medals and the squad from Bolivia were all different teams — three pairwise inequalities.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".
C2!=C3 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".
C1!=C3 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".

% 5. Neither the team from Argentina nor the squad that won 2 silver medals is the squad that won 4 gold medals.
{C="Argentina"; S=2}=0 :- assign(C,S,G), G=4.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), C1="Argentina", S2=2.

% 6. The squad that won 2 gold medals is either the squad that won 6 silver medals or the team from Grenada.
{S=6; C="Grenada"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), G=2.

#show assign/3.
```
</example_2>

Here is the problem to be solved:
<problem_to_solve>
Julia has hired several new people to work as ride-managers for Walton World Amusement Park. She needs to check on their results after their first full day at work. Help her match each new employee to their ride and section of the park (broken down by color), as well as the number of people each served during that day. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. The worker who works in the green section served more riders than Brett.
2. Peter served 25 fewer riders than Willis.
3. Brett served more riders than Peter.
4. The person who works in the yellow section served 25 more riders than the worker who works in the orange section.
5. Peter is either the person who served 125 riders or the person who works in the blue section.

Constants:
employees: Brett; Peter; Victor; Willis.
riders: 50 riders; 75 riders; 100 riders; 125 riders.
section: blue; green; orange; yellow.
</problem_to_solve>

Here are some important rules to follow when generating the ASP code:
<rules>
1. Constant declarations — "category(const1; const2; ...)."
   - Category names: lowercase with underscores only (e.g., wood_type, gold_medals).
   - All constants in the same category must use the same format — all integers or all quoted strings.
   - Prefer integers: strip special characters ($225 → 225), strip units while keeping the number ("3 days" → 3, "2 months" → 2), and convert verbal or ordinal numerals (once → 1, twice → 2, third → 3, September → 9).
   - Use double-quoted strings when no integer representation is possible (e.g., "Bonita", "9:30AM").

2. Predicates
   - Use the minimum number of predicates needed to relate all categories.
   - Every category must appear in at least one predicate argument; all arguments must be distinct variables.
   - Choose a name that reflects the relationship (e.g., match, assign, schedule, launch).

3. Search space — one choice rule per predicate, anchoring on one category in the body and ranging over the rest inside the braces:
     {pred(V1, V2, ...): cat2(V2), cat3(V3), ...}=1 :- cat1(V1).

4. Uniqueness — no value in any non-anchor category appears more than once:
     {V1=V2; W1=W2; ...}=0 :- pred(V1,W1,...), pred(V2,W2,...), (V1,W1,...)!=(V2,W2,...).

5. Constraint rules — encode each clue using one or more rules of these forms, using only defined constants and predicates:
     <C1>; ...; <Cm> :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.      % if all Li hold, at least one Ci must hold
     {<C1>; ...; <Cm>}=k :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.  % if all Li hold, exactly k of Ci hold
     :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.                        % this combination is forbidden

   Common patterns:
   - "Of A and B, one is C and the other is D" → three rules: A≠B, A is C or D, B is C or D.
   - "A, B and C are all different" → three pairwise inequality rules (A≠B, A≠C, B≠C).
   - "Neither A nor B is X" → {A_cond; B_cond}=0 :- ... X ... .

6. Show directives — one "#show pred/arity." per predicate.
</rules>

ASP Program:
asp_generation.thinking:
The user wants an ASP program to solve a logic puzzle.
I need to parse the problem description, extract constants, and translate clues into ASP rules.

**Problem Description:**
- Categories: employees, riders, section.
- Employees: Brett, Peter, Victor, Willis.
- Riders: 50, 75, 100, 125.
- Sections: blue, green, orange, yellow.
- Clues:
  1. The worker who works in the green section served more riders than Brett.
  2. Peter served 25 fewer riders than Willis.
  3. Brett served more riders than Peter.
  4. The person who works in the yellow section served 25 more riders than the worker who works in the orange section.
  5. Peter is either the person who served 125 riders or the person who works in the blue section.

**Constants:**
- employees: "Brett", "Peter", "Victor", "Willis"
- riders: 50, 75, 100, 125
- section: "blue", "green", "orange", "yellow"

**Predicates:**
- Let's use `match(E, R, S)` where E is employee, R is riders, S is section.
- Or maybe `assign(E, R, S)`. I'll stick to `match` or `assign`. `match` is fine.

**Search Space:**
- `match(E, R, S) :- employees(E).`
- For each employee, exactly one rider count and one section.
- `{match(E, R, S): riders(R), section(S)}=1 :- employees(E).`

**Uniqueness:**
- `{E1=E2; R1=R2; S1=S2}=0 :- match(E1,R1,S1), match(E2,R2,S2), (E1,R1,S1)!=(E2,R2,S2).`

**Clues Translation:**
1. "The worker who works in the green section served more riders than Brett."
   - `R1 > R2 :- match(E1, R1, "green"), match(E2, R2, S2), E2="Brett".`
2. "Peter served 25 fewer riders than Willis."
   - `R1 = R2 - 25 :- match(E1, R1, S1), match(E2, R2, S2), E1="Peter", E2="Willis".`
3. "Brett served more riders than Peter."
   - `R1 > R2 :- match(E1, R1, S1), match(E2, R2, S2), E1="Brett", E2="Peter".`
4. "The person who works in the yellow section served 25 more riders than the worker who works in the orange section."
   - `R1 = R2 + 25 :- match(E1, R1, "yellow"), match(E2, R2, "orange").`
5. "Peter is either the person who served 125 riders or the person who works in the blue section."
   - `{R=125; S="blue"}=1 :- match(E, R, S), E="Peter".`

Let's double check clues and constraints.
Riders: 50, 75, 100, 125.
Clue 2: Peter = Willis - 25. Possible pairs: (50, 75), (75, 100), (100, 125).
Clue 3: Brett > Peter.
Clue 1: Green > Brett.
So Green > Brett > Peter.
Riders are 50, 75, 100, 125.
If Peter=50, Brett=75, Green=100 or 125.
If Peter=75, Brett=100, Green=125.
If Peter=100, Brett=125, Green=? (none left)
So Peter must be 50 or 75.
Clue 5: Peter is either 125 or Blue.
Since Peter can't be 125 (because Peter < Brett < Green <= 125), Peter must be Blue.
So Peter works in Blue section.
Let's check clue 4: Yellow = Orange + 25. Pairs: (50, 75), (75, 100), (100, 125).
Since Peter is Blue and serves 50 or 75 riders.
If Peter serves 75, then Orange cannot be 75. So Orange=50, Yellow=75. But Peter=75, so Peter would be Yellow? But Peter is Blue. Contradiction.
So Peter must serve 50 riders.
Then Orange=50, Yellow=75. But Peter serves 50, so Peter is Orange? But Peter is Blue. Contradiction?
Wait, let's re-read clue 4 carefully: "The person who works in the yellow section served 25 more riders than the worker who works in the orange section."
This means `R_yellow = R_orange + 25`.
If Peter serves 50, he is Blue. So Orange != 50.
Possible rider assignments:
Peter = 50 (Blue)
Willis = 75
Brett > Peter -> Brett could be 75, 100, 125. But Willis=75, so Brett=100 or 125.
Green > Brett.
If Brett=100, Green=125.
Then riders: Peter=50, Willis=75, Brett=100, Victor=125.
Sections: Peter=Blue. Green=Victor (125).
Yellow = Orange + 25. Remaining sections: Green, Orange, Yellow.
Green is Victor (125). So Yellow and Orange are Brett and Willis.
But Yellow = Orange + 25. 100 = 75 + 25. So Brett (100) is Yellow, Willis (75) is Orange.
Check: Green (Victor, 125) > Brett (100) -> True.
Peter (50) < Brett (100) -> True.
Peter (50) = Willis (75) - 25 -> True.
Yellow (100) = Orange (75) + 25 -> True.
Peter is Blue -> True.
All clues satisfied!
Solution:
Brett: 100 riders, Yellow section.
Peter: 50 riders, Blue section.
Victor: 125 riders, Green section.
Willis: 75 riders, Orange section.

Let's verify Clue 5: "Peter is either the person who served 125 riders or the person who works in the blue section." -> Peter works in blue. Correct.
Everything matches.

Now, translate to ASP.

Constants:
`employees("Brett"; "Peter"; "Victor"; "Willis").`
`riders(50; 75; 100; 125).`
`section("blue"; "green"; "orange"; "yellow").`

Search space:
`{match(E, R, S): riders(R), section(S)}=1 :- employees(E).`

Uniqueness:
`{E1=E2; R1=R2; S1=S2}=0 :- match(E1,R1,S1), match(E2,R2,S2), (E1,R1,S1)!=(E2,R2,S2).`

Clues:
1. `R1 > R2 :- match(E1, R1, "green"), match(E2, R2, S2), E2="Brett".`
2. `R1 = R2 - 25 :- match(E1, R1, S1), match(E2, R2, S2), E1="Peter", E2="Willis".`
3. `R1 > R2 :- match(E1, R1, S1), match(E2, R2, S2), E1="Brett", E2="Peter".`
4. `R1 = R2 + 25 :- match(E1, R1, "yellow"), match(E2, R2, "orange").`
5. `{R=125; S="blue"}=1 :- match(E, R, S), E="Peter".`

Show directive:
`#show match/3.`

Check rules format:
- "Category names: lowercase with underscores only" -> `riders`, `section`, `employees`.
- "All constants in the same category must use the same format" -> integers for riders, strings for others.
- "Search space — one choice rule per predicate, anchoring on one category in the body and ranging over the rest inside the braces" -> `{match(E, R, S): riders(R), section(S)}=1 :- employees(E).`
- "Uniqueness — no value in any non-anchor category appears more than once" -> `{E1=E2; R1=R2; S1=S2}=0 :- match(E1,R1,S1), match(E2,R2,S2), (E1,R1,S1)!=(E2,R2,S2).`
- "Constraint rules — encode each clue using one or more rules of these forms..." -> All clues are positive constraints or integrity constraints? Actually, clues 1-4 are positive facts/rules that hold. Clue 5 is a disjunction. I will encode them as rules that must be satisfied. In ASP, `:-` is for constraints (forbidden), but here we want to assert relationships. The prompt says: `<C1>; ...; <Cm> :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.` or `{<C1>; ...; <Cm>}=k :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.` This means if L holds, then C must hold. So I will use `:-` in the reverse direction? Wait, the prompt says:
`<C1>; ...; <Cm> :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.` means "if Li hold, then at least one Ci holds". This is a constraint on the head. In ASP, `head :- body.` means if body is true, head must be true. So `R1 > R2 :- match(...), E2="Brett".` is correct.
Wait, Clingo evaluates `head :- body.` as a constraint if head is false? No, `head :- body.` is a normal rule. It forces `head` to be true when `body` is true. If `head` cannot be true, the model is invalid. So it works as a constraint.

Let's check the exact syntax for inequalities in ASP. `R1 > R2` is fine.
Clue 1: `R1 > R2 :- match(E1, R1, "green"), match(E2, R2, S2), E2="Brett".`
Clue 2: `R1 = R2 - 25 :- match(E1, R1, S1), match(E2, R2, S2), E1="Peter", E2="Willis".`
Clue 3: `R1 > R2 :- match(E1, R1, S1), match(E2, R2, S2), E1="Brett", E2="Peter".`
Clue 4: `R1 = R2 + 25 :- match(E1, R1, "yellow"), match(E2, R2, "orange").`
Clue 5: `{R=125; S="blue"}=1 :- match(E, R, S), E="Peter".`

Wait, Clue 4 says "The person who works in the yellow section served 25 more riders than the worker who works in the orange section."
This implies there is exactly one person in yellow and one in orange.
`R1 = R2 + 25 :- match(E1, R1, "yellow"), match(E2, R2, "orange").` is correct.

One minor point: Clue 1 says "The worker who works in the green section served more riders than Brett."
`R1 > R2 :- match(E1, R1, "green"), match(E2, R2, S2), E2="Brett".`
This is correct.

Let's assemble the program.

```asp
% Constants
employees("Brett"; "Peter"; "Victor"; "Willis").
riders(50; 75; 100; 125).
section("blue"; "green"; "orange"; "yellow").

% Search space
{match(E, R, S): riders(R), section(S)}=1 :- employees(E).

% Uniqueness
{E1=E2; R1=R2; S1=S2}=0 :- match(E1,R1,S1), match(E2,R2,S2), (E1,R1,S1)!=(E2,R2,S2).

% 1. The worker who works in the green section served more riders than Brett.
R1 > R2 :- match(E1, R1, "green"), match(E2, R2, S2), E2="Brett".

% 2. Peter served 25 fewer riders than Willis.
R1 = R2 - 25 :- match(E1, R1, S1), match(E2, R2, S2), E1="Peter", E2="Willis".

% 3. Brett served more riders than Peter.
R1 > R2 :- match(E1, R1, S1), match(E2, R2, S2), E1="Brett", E2="Peter".

% 4. The person who works in the yellow section served 25 more riders than the worker who works in the orange section.
R1 = R2 + 25 :- match(E1, R1, "yellow"), match(E2, R2, "orange").

% 5. Peter is either the person who served 125 riders or the person who works in the blue section.
{R=125; S="blue"}=1 :- match(E, R, S), E="Peter".

#show match/3.
```

Check if any clues need to be encoded as constraints (forbidden combinations) rather than positive rules.
The prompt says: `<C1>; ...; <Cm> :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.`
This matches my encoding.
Wait, clue 1: "The worker who works in the green section served more riders than Brett."
My rule: `R1 > R2 :- match(E1, R1, "green"), match(E2, R2, S2), E2="Brett".`
This says: for the person in green and Brett, the riders of green > riders of Brett. Correct.

Clue 2: "Peter served 25 fewer riders than Willis."
`R1 = R2 - 25 :- match(E1, R1, S1), match(E2, R2, S2), E1="Peter", E2="Willis".`
Correct.

Clue 3: "Brett served more riders than Peter."
`R1 > R2 :- match(E1, R1, S1), match(E2, R2, S2), E1="Brett", E2="Peter".`
Correct.

Clue 4: "The person who works in the yellow section served 25 more riders than the worker who works in the orange section."
`R1 = R2 + 25 :- match(E1, R1, "yellow"), match(E2, R2, "orange").`
Correct.

Clue 5: "Peter is either the person who served 125 riders or the person who works in the blue section."
`{R=125; S="blue"}=1 :- match(E, R, S), E="Peter".`
Correct.

One thing to check: Clingo's syntax for `=` in head with arithmetic. `R1 = R2 - 25` is fine.
Also, `R1 > R2` is fine.
The uniqueness rule uses `{E1=E2; R1=R2; S1=S2}=0`. This is a standard way to enforce all-different in Clingo.
Wait, the prompt says: `{V1=V2; W1=W2; ...}=0 :- pred(V1,W1,...), pred(V2,W2,...), (V1,W1,...)!=(V2,W2,...).`
This matches exactly.

Let's verify the constants format.
`riders(50; 75; 100; 125).` -> integers.
`section("blue"; "green"; "orange"; "yellow").` -> strings.
`employees("Brett"; "Peter"; "Victor"; "Willis").` -> strings.
All good.

One minor point: In Clue 4, "The person who works in the yellow section served 25 more riders than the worker who works in the orange section."
Does it imply `R_yellow = R_orange + 25`? Yes.
Could it be encoded as `:- match(E1, R1, "yellow"), match(E2, R2, "orange"), R1 != R2 + 25.`?
The prompt prefers `<C1>; ...; <Cm> :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.`
So `R1 = R2 + 25 :- match(E1, R1, "yellow"), match(E2, R2, "orange").` is correct.

Wait, should I use `!=` in the head? No, `=` is fine.
In Clingo, `R1 = R2 + 25` is valid.

Let's double check Clue 1: "The worker who works in the green section served more riders than Brett."
`R1 > R2 :- match(E1, R1, "green"), match(E2, R2, S2), E2="Brett".`
This is correct.

Check Clue 5: "Peter is either the person who served 125 riders or the person who works in the blue section."
`{R=125; S="blue"}=1 :- match(E, R, S), E="Peter".`
This says exactly one of these holds. Correct.

Is there any missing constraint? "no option in any category will ever be used more than once." -> handled by uniqueness rule.
"Search space — one choice rule per predicate, anchoring on one category in the body and ranging over the rest inside the braces" -> handled.

One detail: In example 2, they used `assign/3`. I'll use `match/3`.
The prompt says "Choose a name that reflects the relationship (e.g., match, assign, schedule, launch)."
I'll stick with `match`.

Let's test the logic mentally again.
Clue 2: Peter = Willis - 25.
Clue 3: Brett > Peter.
Clue 1: Green > Brett.
So Green > Brett > Peter.
Riders: 50, 75, 100, 125.
Possible for (Green, Brett, Peter): (100, 75, 50) or (125, 100, 75).
If (125, 100, 75), then Victor = 50.
Clue 4: Yellow = Orange + 25.
Remaining riders for Yellow/Orange: 50 and 100? No, 100 is Brett. If Brett is 100, he can't be both Yellow and Orange.

Yellow = Orange + 25. Pairs from {50, 75, 100, 125} left: (75, 100) or (100, 125) or (50, 75).
But Peter=75, Brett=100, Green=125.
So Orange and Yellow must be Victor(50) and Peter(75)? But Peter is not necessarily Orange/Yellow.
Actually, sections are assigned to employees.
We have 4 employees: Brett, Peter, Victor, Willis.
Sections: blue, green, orange, yellow.
If Peter=75, Brett=100, Green=125 (Victor), Willis=50.
Then Yellow = Orange + 25.
Available riders for Yellow/Orange: 50(Willis) and 75(Peter). 75 = 50 + 25. So Yellow=Peter(75), Orange=Willis(50).
But Peter is Yellow. Clue 5: Peter is either 125 or Blue. Peter is 75, so he must be Blue. Contradiction.
So Peter cannot be 75.
Thus Peter=50, Brett=75, Green=100 or 125.
If Green=100, then Brett=75, Peter=50. Willis must be 125.
Check Clue 2: Peter = Willis - 25 -> 50 = 125 - 25? False.
So Green cannot be 100.
Thus Green=125. Brett=100, Peter=50. Willis=75.
Check Clue 2: 50 = 75 - 25. True.
Check Clue 3: 100 > 50. True.
Check Clue 1: Green(125) > Brett(100). True.
Check Clue 4: Yellow = Orange + 25. Remaining riders: 75(Willis), 100(Brett). 100 = 75 + 25. So Yellow=Brett(100), Orange=Willis(75).
Check Clue 5: Peter is 125 or Blue. Peter is 50, so must be Blue.
Sections: Peter=Blue, Brett=Yellow, Willis=Orange, Victor=Green.
All consistent.

The ASP code should find this unique model.

One thing to note: In Clingo, `R1 = R2 - 25` might be evaluated as arithmetic. It's fine.
Sometimes it's safer to write `R2 = R1 + 25` to avoid negative numbers, but riders are positive anyway.
I'll keep `R1 = R2 - 25`.

Let's verify the exact format of the prompt's examples.
Example 1:
```asp
% 2. The item made of poplar costs more than Yvette's piece.
P1>P2 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), W1="poplar", E2="Yvette".
```
Example 2:

```asp
% Constants
employees("Brett"; "Peter"; "Victor"; "Willis").
riders(50; 75; 100; 125).
section("blue"; "green"; "orange"; "yellow").

% Search space: for each employee E, assign exactly one rider count R and section S.
{match(E, R, S): riders(R), section(S)}=1 :- employees(E).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{E1=E2; R1=R2; S1=S2}=0 :- match(E1,R1,S1), match(E2,R2,S2), (E1,R1,S1)!=(E2,R2,S2).

% 1. The worker who works in the green section served more riders than Brett.
R1 > R2 :- match(E1, R1, "green"), match(E2, R2, S2), E2="Brett".

% 2. Peter served 25 fewer riders than Willis.
R1 = R2 - 25 :- match(E1, R1, S1), match(E2, R2, S2), E1="Peter", E2="Willis".

% 3. Brett served more riders than Peter.
R1 > R2 :- match(E1, R1, S1), match(E2, R2, S2), E1="Brett", E2="Peter".

% 4. The person who works in the yellow section served 25 more riders than the worker who works in the orange section.
R1 = R2 + 25 :- match(E1, R1, "yellow"), match(E2, R2, "orange").

% 5. Peter is either the person who served 125 riders or the person who works in the blue section.
{R=125; S="blue"}=1 :- match(E, R, S), E="Peter".

#show match/3.
```

% 1. The worker who works in the green section served more riders than Brett.

% 2. Peter served 25 fewer riders than Willis.

section("blue"; "green"; "orange"; "yellow").

{match(E, R, S): riders(R), section(S)}=1 :- employees(E).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{E1=E2; R1=R2; S1=S2}=0 :- match(E1,R1,S1), match(E2,R2,S2), (E1,R1,S1)!=(E2,R2,S2).

% 1. The worker who works in the green section served more riders than Brett.
R1 > R2 :- match(E1, R1, "green"), match(E2, R2, S2), E2="Brett".

% 2. Peter served 25 fewer riders than Willis.
R1 = R2 - 25 :- match(E1, R1, S1), match(E2, R2, S2), E1="Peter", E2="Willis".

% 3. Brett served more riders than Peter.
